text.tspan: 09. 528. Random Pick with Weight (Medium)
A: Given a an array of weights W = [w1,..,wn]
let sumW be the sum of elements of W.
Let P be an array of accumulated probabilities
defined as:
P = [p1,..,pn] where pi = pi-1 + wi / sumW
To return a random index from [0, n-1] weighted
by w, generate a random number x between 0 and 1,
and return the index i of the first element of P
such that x < pi.
If linear search is used to find this pi, the
complexity of this solution becomes O(n), but it
can be speed up to O(log(n)) if binary search is
used.
Example:
Assuming x = random() = 0.3, then the smallest
i that satisfies x < P[i] is 1. Thus the algo-
rithm returns 1.
10. 227. Basic Calculator II (Medium)
A: Given a string s representing a mathematical
expression, traverse the characters of s keeping
a stack of intermediate expression results, an
auxiliary variable op to hold the next operation
to be performed and num to keep the latest number
parsed from s.
While traversing, if we find a character c corres-
ponding to an operator +, -, *, /, we apply the
the operator in op to between the stack and num,
(see applyOp(op, num, stack)), set op = c and
reset num to zero.
When the traversal is finished, apply op to what-
ever was left in the stack and the last value in
num. Finally, add the values in the stack and
return the sum.
def applyOp(op, num, stack):
if op == "+": stack.append(num)
elif op == "-": stack.append(-num)
elif op == "*": stack.append(stack.pop() * num)
elif op == "/": stack.append(int(stack.pop() / num))
The idea is to use the stack to enforce the
order of operations.
11. 215. Kth Largest Element in an Array (Medium)
A: Use a minHeap and fill it with the elements of
arr until it has k elements, from then on replace
the top of minHeap with any subsequent element
arr[i] such that arr[i] > minHeap[0]. In the end
this will put the kth largest element of arr
on the top of the minHeap, return minHeap[0].
Example:
Given:
Then
Thus the kth largest element in arr is:
minHeap[0] = 3
12. 938. Range Sum of BST (Easy)
A: Recursively traverse the BST comparing the
value of each node with low and high.
If node.val < low it means that the values of all
the nodes to the left of node are also less than
low thus we can skip them from the sum, continue
exploring the right branch of the node.
Analogously, if node.val > high it means that the
values of all the nodes to the right of node are
greater than high, this also puts them out of
range, continue exploring the left branch of the
node.
Finally, if low <= node.val <= high we have found
a node with a value within range, add node.val
to the sum of values in range and explore both
left and right branches.
In Python this is writen like this:
def rangeSumOfBST(root, low, high):
if not root:
return 0
if root.val < low:
return rangeSumOfBST(root.right, low, high)
if root.val > high:
return rangeSumOfBST(root.left, low, high)
return root.val+rangeSumOfBST(root.left, low, high)+\
rangeSumOfBST(root.right, low, high)
Example:
Then rangeSumOfBST(bst, low, high) will return 18
